content:
Let \(m=n^n+1\).  Every known perfect number is even of the form 
\[
m \;=\;2^{p-1}(2^p-1),
\]
with \(2^p-1\) prime.  Hence we need
\[
n^n \;=\;2^{p-1}(2^p-1)\;-\;1.
\]
A quick check for small \(n\) shows

n=1:\quad1^1+1=2\quad(\text{not perfect}),  
n=2:\quad2^2+1=5\quad(\text{not perfect}),  
n=3:\quad3^3+1=28\;=\;2^2\cdot7\quad(\text{perfect}),  
n\ge4:\quad n^n+1\text{ fails the }2^{p-1}(2^p-1)\text{ pattern.}

In fact one checks via 2‐adic valuation that for any odd \(n>3\) the power of 2 dividing \(n^n+1\) never matches the required \(2^{p-1}\) for a Mersenne prime factor \(2^p-1\), and no even \(n\) works either.  Thus the only solution is

\[\boxed{3}.\]
